title: PR Society Delhi Announces New Governing Board 2026–28; GS Bawa Elected Chairman of PRSD - Daily Pioneer
content: The Public Relations Society, Delhi (PRSD), the Capital’s premier forum for PR, Corporate Communication, and Media Professionals, ushered in its new Governing Board during the National Public Relations Day celebrations held at the Press Club of India. The entire Executive Committee for the year 2026-2028 term was declared elected unopposed. The results were formally announced by the Returning Officer before a distinguished gathering of industry leaders, senior communicators, veterans, and young professionals. A veteran communication strategist, Bawa has been elevated to the apex role after serving the Society with distinction as General Secretary in the previous term. He will be joined by Dr. Jyoti Kumar, Chief General Manager (Corporate Communications), GAIL (India) Ltd., who takes charge as Secretary. The core leadership team is further strengthened by Rama Vijay, former General Manager (Corporate Communications), REC Ltd - Vice Chairman, Dr Akshat Chopra, Deputy Manager (Corporate Communications), Power Grid Corporation of India Ltd as Joint Secretary, and Vipin Kharbanda - Treasurer. The team brings together decades of cross-sectoral corporate expertise, policy advocacy, and institutional knowledge to lead PRSD into its next phase of growth. Jyoti Kumar, Secretary, said in a fast-evolving, high-velocity media ecosystem, the communicator’s role is more critical than ever. PRSD will act as a catalyst for upskilling, ethical practice, and thought leadership. We will create more focused, interactive platforms for professionals to learn, share, and lead, Jyoti said. Founded as a professional body of public relations and corporate communication practitioners, PRSD is dedicated to advancing the profession through knowledge-sharing, capacity building, and advocacy.  It serves as a bridge between industry, academia, and policymakers to strengthen the strategic role of communication in nation-building. title: Home Shopping and SSM Join Forces to Target 40-60 Female Shoppers - Seoul Economic Daily
content:
GS The Fresh and GS Shop Launch Collaboration in January
Focused Targeting of Female Customers in Their 40s-60s
Points Awarded to Customers Using Both Channels
Monthly Average Payment Jumps 29% Home shopping operators and corporate-run supermarkets (SSM) are accelerating synergy efforts by launching joint strategies centered on their core customer base of women in their 40s to 60s. With both industries facing profitability pressures, they are breaking down barriers between channels to maximize customer lock-in effects. Hlim Group's recent bid to acquire Homeplus Express is also seen as a move aimed at securing business synergies through linkage with its subsidiary NS Home Shopping. According to industry sources on Thursday, GS Retail's (007070.KS) SSM chain "GS The Fresh" and TV home shopping channel "GS Shop" have built the industry's first full-scale collaboration framework. The two units are expanding cross-channel purchases through joint customer relationship management (CRM) marketing based on customer data accumulated via their integrated membership program. Starting in January this year, the companies introduced benefits offering additional points to customers who use both channels simultaneously. The flagship "15Days" event runs from the start of each month through the 15th, awarding points to customers who participate after paying with GS Pay at both GS Shop and GS The Fresh. This month, linked promotions have been further strengthened, including offering GS The Fresh discount coupons to customers who participate in the GS Shop app attendance event. The joint marketing has produced tangible results. Compared to December last year, the monthly average payment amount among event participants rose 29.0% in the first quarter of this year, while the number of participating customers increased 23.3%. Average spending per customer also climbed 4.8% during the same period, making cross-channel synergy effects visible. The push behind GS Retail's strategy reflects an intent to overcome slowing profitability in both the home shopping and SSM businesses. Despite top-line growth, profit metrics have fallen short of expectations. GS The Fresh posted operating profit of just 27.1 billion won ($19.6 million) last year despite continued revenue growth, with an operating margin of only 1.6%. GS Shop has also seen its earnings decline, with operating profit falling below 100 billion won. Overlapping customer bases between the two businesses are cited as another key driver behind the deepening cooperation. Industry observers expect significant integrated marketing benefits from shared customer data, given that both home shopping and supermarkets draw a high proportion of women aged 40 to 60. "The strategy aims to strengthen customer lock-in by lowering barriers between channels to increase cross-purchases and encourage repeat buying by the same customers," a retail industry official said. The scope of collaboration is expanding beyond simple promotions into business integration. A notable example is the planned rollout of an "omnichannel" service that will allow customers to pick up items ordered on GS Shop at nearby GS The Fresh stores. Observers expect the cooperation model to evolve further by combining quick commerce to organically connect offline fresh food with broadcast and mobile channels. Industry watchers view Hlim Group's bid to acquire Homeplus Express as similarly aimed at leveraging the 293 Homeplus Express stores nationwide to build an instant delivery system, expand product competitiveness and broaden customer touchpoints to generate synergies between the two channels. "The combination of home shopping and SSM is a core means of transforming the business model, enabling expansion into the short-distance instant delivery market and securing omnichannel competitiveness based on online-offline integration," an NS Home Shopping official said. "In particular, while broadcast time constraints have limited the expansion of private brand (PB) products, securing offline hubs will allow us to absorb inventory that could not be cleared through home shopping and fully develop related businesses," the official added. title: New BMW F 450 GS arrives in Brazil with 4 versions and surprises by revealing differences that can completely change your choice between Basic, Exclusive, Sport, and GS Trophy. - CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
content: The arrival of the new BMW F 450 GS in Brazil is already starting to stir the adventure motorcycle market, especially because the model comes with a clear proposal: to replace the G 310 line and raise the category’s standard. Furthermore, the German manufacturer is betting on a strategy that expands the bike’s reach, offering four distinct versions — Basic, Exclusive, Sport, and GS Trophy — each designed for a specific type of rider. The information was released by “Motor1.com”, which detailed the main differences between the versions of the new trail bike, highlighting how BMW aims to cater to everyone from beginners to more demanding motorcyclists looking for advanced performance and technology. In this context, understanding the differences between these versions is essential, especially for those looking to invest in a motorcycle that balances cost-benefit, technology, and performance. Chinese automaker starts operations with electrified vehicles and is betting on an industrial partnership to accelerate national production, repositioning Brazil on the global map of the automotive industry. Week moves automotive market with BYD Mako hybrid flex produced in Brazil, Jeep Avenger already on the streets, Volkswagen ID. Polo electric, and news from Agrishow 2026. An electric motorcycle with artificial intelligence and an aerospace gyroscope stands up on its own, corrects falls before the rider notices, and already has pre-sales scheduled for May. The OMO X promises to end the nightmare that kills thousands of motorcyclists every year at traffic lights and on curves. New Chinese brands arrive in Brazil in 2026 and threaten BYD’s dominance with cheaper electric cars, advanced technology, and an aggressive strategy in the national automotive market. To begin, the Basic version of the BMW F 450 GS is the entry point of the line and was designed for those seeking a versatile motorcycle without excessive equipment. Even so, despite being the simplest, it maintains a solid technological base, which already places it above many competitors. Among its main highlights are the alloy wheels and mixed-use tires, which ensure good performance on both asphalt and light trails. In addition, full LED lighting and a 6.5-inch TFT screen reinforce the model’s modern character. On the other hand, it is important to note that the bidirectional quickshifter does not come standard in this version, being only optional. In other words, it is an ideal motorcycle for those who want to enter the adventure universe without paying for extra features they might not use frequently. Moving up the line, the Exclusive version already delivers a more complete package. In this case, BMW adds items that make a difference, especially on long trips and more intense use. Among the additional equipment, highlights include: Thus, the motorcycle becomes more comfortable and safer, especially for those intending to tackle long distances or varied terrains. The Sport version, on the other hand, retains practically all the items of the Exclusive, but brings an important differential: optimized suspension. This ensures greater control and stability on more challenging terrains, making it more suitable for riders seeking superior performance. Furthermore, the exclusive red paint reinforces the sporty look, adding personality to the model. Finally, the GS Trophy version represents the pinnacle of the new BMW F 450 GS. Directly inspired by the iconic BMW R 1300 GS, it brings together the most advanced in design, technology, and performance. Additionally, this version features all available riding modes, including Enduro Pro, designed for more extreme off-road use. Therefore, it is an option aimed at more experienced motorcyclists who seek maximum performance combined with cutting-edge technology. Regardless of the chosen version, the BMW F 450 GS shares a robust and modern mechanical base. The motorcycle is produced by the Indian TVS and equipped with a 420 cc parallel twin-cylinder liquid-cooled engine. Furthermore, the tubular steel chassis, combined with inverted front suspension and a rear monoshock, ensures a balance between comfort and performance. Another strong point is the technological package, which includes: With this, the new 450 GS positions itself as one of the most complete motorcycles in its category. Given this scenario, it is clear that the BMW F 450 GS arrives in Brazil with a solid and highly competitive proposal. By offering four distinct versions, the brand expands its reach and allows different rider profiles to find an option suited to their needs. Whether for urban use, long trips, or off-road adventures, the new trail promises to deliver performance, technology, and versatility. Which version of the BMW F 450 GS would you choose for your riding style, a simpler one or the complete GS Trophy? title: Song Min-hyuk Wins GS Caltex Maekyung Open in Playoff - 조선일보
content: Defeats Jo Min-kyu amid Korea Golf Association score correction controversy Song Min-hyuk, 22 years old, claimed the GS Caltex Maeil Open champion title after an extension. In his third year as a professional, he achieved his first victory and secured prize money of 300 million Korean won. On the 3rd, during the fourth round held at Nam Seoul CC (par 71) in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province, Song Min-hyuk reduced his score by one stroke with three birdies and two bogeys. With a final total of 11-under-par 273 strokes, he entered a playoff with Jo Min-kyu, 38 years old, and defeated him on the first playoff hole—the 18th—by securing a par while Jo Min-kyu could only manage a bogey. Song Min-hyuk trailed leader Jo Min-kyu by three strokes after a bogey on the 16th hole (par 4), appearing likely to lose. However, Jo Min-kyu lost three strokes on the final two holes, giving Song the chance to extend into a playoff. Jo Min-kyu could have secured the victory with a bogey on the 18th hole (par 4), but he missed a 1-meter putt and recorded a double bogey. Ultimately, he finished as runner-up for the fourth time at the Maeil Open after losing in the playoff. The day’s competition concluded in disarray due to the Korea Golf Association’s lax and careless management. When the champion group finished the 18th hole, the leaderboard showed three players—Song Min-hyuk, Jo Min-kyu, and Heo In-hoe—tied at 11-under-par for first place. However, when the playoff began, Heo In-hoe did not appear at the 18th hole tee box. This was because, just before the playoff began, Heo In-hoe’s final score was corrected to 9-under-par, placing him in a tie for third. This was due to a change in his score on the seventh hole during the third round the previous day, which was adjusted from par to double bogey. On the 2nd, Heo In-hoe hit a provisional ball after his tee shot on the seventh hole headed toward the right out-of-bounds (OB) area. He then went to the OB area to look for the original ball, but the "fore caddie" who determines the ball’s landing point picked up Heo’s ball and handed it to the tournament officials, causing the issue. The fore caddie claimed the original ball was in the OB area, but some spectators argued it was not. As a result, the referee (tournament official) allowed Heo to play his second shot from the provisional ball on the fairway without penalty. Heo recorded a par on that hole. A source from the Korea Golf Association explained, "Since the ball had disappeared, if they confirmed the original ball was not in the OB area, they should have estimated the landing point and allowed play from there without penalty. If they judged it as OB, they should have imposed a two-stroke penalty and allowed the provisional ball to be played. The referee made a mistake." They added, "Initially, due to insufficient evidence, we accepted the par, but after reviewing additional testimonies and reports from officials, we corrected the score for the seventh hole in the third round to a double bogey." There is a rule in golf that states "incorrect rulings can be corrected before the conclusion of the tournament." The association informed Heo of the score change after he had already started the fourth round and completed his play. On the same day, at the KLPGA (Korean Women’s Professional Golf Association) Tour DB Women’s Championship held at Rainbow Hills CC in Eumseong, Chungcheongbuk Province, Yoo Hyun-jo, 21 years old, won with a score of 7-under-par 281 and received prize money of 216 million Korean won.

title: He was thrilled to win his first professional title at a competition that was considered the "best a.. - 매일경제
content:
He was thrilled to win his first professional title at a competition that was considered the "best amateur" when he was a national team member. Song Min-hyuk (22), a rising male golf player, won the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open (total prize money of 1.3 billion won) and succeeded in winning his first professional title. Song Min-hyuk cut one shot in the final fourth round of the tournament held at Nam Seoul CC (par 71) in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 3rd, tied with Cho Min-gyu with 11-under 273 overall. He then beat Cho Min-gyu, who blocked it with par in the first overtime at the 18th hole (par 4), and won. Song Min-hyuk, who made his debut in the Korea Professional Golf Association (KPGA) Tour in 2024, won his first championship in his third year at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. Song Min-hyuk received 300 million won in prize money for this victory. At the same time, he cheered for the second year of the Asian Tour and the fifth year of the KPGA Tour. The tournament was co-organized by the Korea Golf Association and the Asian Tour, and 138 players representing the KPGA Tour and the Asian Tour competed for shots. Song Min-hyuk, who tied for 16th place in the 41st tournament in 2022 and became the "best amateur" in the tournament, showed a strong performance at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open by finishing second at the 42nd tournament in 2023. He made his professional debut in 2024, and won the rookie of the year with the top 10 in the KPGA Tour four times that year. Last year, Song Min-hyuk, who played in 20 competitions and ranked in the top 10 seven times but did not win, finally lifted the trophy after his third competition this season. Song Min-hyuk suffered a sore throat during the first round and had a hard time. Song Min-hyuk, who had been taking painkillers and taking lower-body baths, finally tied for fifth place in the second round and reduced five shots in the third round to tie for the lead. In the final fourth round, Song Min-hyuk raised the mood with consecutive birdies in the 3rd and 4th holes. There was also a crisis. He made a bogey with three putts on the 16th hole (par 4), falling to three strokes behind Cho Min-gyu. However, a reversal unfolded on the 17th hole (par 3). Cho Min-gyu, who wrote a bogey with two putts in the hole, made a double bogey with three putts following a tee shot mistake on the 18th hole (par 4). Song Min-hyuk, who blocked holes 17 and 18 with par, took the game to extra time. In the 18th hole, Song finally turned the tables. Song Min-hyuk sent his tee shot straight to the fairway, then put his second shot on the green and made a two-putt save. On the other hand, Cho Min-gyu, who was pushed to the left of the fairway from the tee shot, failed to protect the par in the end and lowered his head again. Cho Min-gyu finished second in the tournament for the fourth time in his career after 2011, 2020, and 2022. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: BMW R1300 GS Adventure Dark Edition: France-Only Special at 22,900 Euros - motorcycles.news
content:
BMW Motorrad France already attracted attention last year with exclusive special models of the F 900 GS and R 1300 GS for the French market. Now comes the next round: The R 1300 GS Adventure, the flagship of the GS lineup, gets its own Dark Edition. The strategy behind it is the same as with its predecessors: An upgraded overall package with black aesthetics and additional equipment that is more attractively priced than an individually configured standard version. The Dark Edition thus targets riders looking for a fully equipped GS Adventure with a distinctive appearance without having to work through the configurator. The Dark Edition is based on the Triple Black concept that BMW has already used in previous special models. The Black Storm Metallic paint is complemented by completely black spoked wheels. The tank panels are also painted black, while the aluminum tank features a brushed finish without visible weld seams. A tall, tinted windscreen and tinted deflectors complete the dark overall impression. Beyond the aesthetics, the Dark Edition also includes functional extras that are optional on the standard version. These include heated comfort seats for rider and pillion as well as an alarm system. The full standard equipment of the regular R 1300 GS Adventure is retained: Dynamic Cruise Control (adaptive cruise control), Dynamic Traction Control, ABS Pro Full Integral, four riding modes (Eco, Rain, Road, Enduro), heated grips, LED auxiliary headlights, Keyless Ride and the intelligent emergency call system. The list price of the Dark Edition is 25,600 euros (approximately 29,950 USD). However, BMW Motorrad France offers a customer discount of 2,700 euros (approximately 3,160 USD), which can be used either as a discount on the purchase price or as a credit toward rider equipment and genuine accessories. This brings the effective price down to 22,900 euros (approximately 26,800 USD). The Dark Edition thus costs around 1,700 euros (approximately 1,990 USD) more than the standard R 1300 GS Adventure in France, but offers additional equipment that would be more expensive when configured individually. Alternatively, BMW France offers a long-term lease over 36 months: 299 euros per month with no down payment at a mileage allowance of 25,000 kilometers including extended warranty. BMW Motorrad France has been pursuing an independent special model strategy since 2025, tailored exclusively to the French market. Last year, an F 900 GS Dark Edition and an R 1300 GS Dark Edition were already released, both of which were likewise available only in France. Shortly before the Adventure Dark Edition, BMW Motorrad France also introduced special models of the F 900 R in Frozen Grey and the F 900 XR in Aurelius Green, whose color schemes are inspired by the corporation’s automotive division. According to Grigory Alaev, specialist for product communication and lifestyle at BMW Motorrad France, the new Dark Edition benefits from the same customer discount as the current offer on all GS Adventure models in the French dealer network. Whether BMW encourages other national subsidiaries to pursue similar initiatives is unknown. The fact that France appears to be successful with this strategy at least suggests that comparable approaches would also be conceivable in other markets. BMW South Africa pursues a similar concept of market-exclusive special models with the recently introduced M 1000 RR and S 1000 RR M2 Editions. The R 1300 GS Adventure established itself as the best-selling model in BMW’s entire motorcycle portfolio in 2025. A total of 33,570 units were sold worldwide, with the Adventure variant even surpassing the R 1300 GS with its 32,555 units. In the United Kingdom, the GSA led the sales statistics in the adventure segment in seven out of twelve months. The positioning as a bestseller makes the release of special models understandable: BMW can maintain interest in an already successful model with limited editions like the Dark Edition while simultaneously increasing perceived value without having to make technical changes to the production version. The Dark Edition is not a technical advancement at its core, but rather an equipment package with a distinctive visual identity. Anyone who would configure an R 1300 GS Adventure with heated seats, alarm system and the Triple Black look anyway gets a better deal with the Dark Edition package. For those interested outside of France, however, the only options remain the regular configurator or the used market. The ordering period runs from April 1 to June 30, 2026, with delivery required by July 31, 2026. Given the high demand for the GS Adventure, the limited Dark Edition is unlikely to remain available for long. The Dark Edition has a list price of 25,600 euros (approximately 29,950 USD). No. The Dark Edition is exclusively intended for the French market and can only be ordered through the French BMW dealer network. Buyers outside France can only access comparable equipment through the standard configurator or the used market. The Dark Edition adds heated comfort seats for rider and pillion, an alarm system, Black Storm Metallic paint, black spoked wheels, black-painted tank panels, a brushed aluminum tank, a tall tinted windscreen and tinted deflectors to the standard equipment of the R 1300 GS Adventure. title: The GS Caltex Maekyung Open, the "Golf Festival in May" with the gallery, excited golf fans with hot.. - 매일경제
content:
The GS Caltex Maekyung Open, the "Golf Festival in May" with the gallery, excited golf fans with hot prizes during the tournament. Among the prizes offered by professional golfers, Park Sang-hyun, who has the most wins (2 wins) in his career at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, gave a unique memory to the winner with a caddy bag and a "surprise gift." Kim Hyung-chul (59), who visited Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 3rd, when the final fourth round of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open was held, smiled happily with "jackpot luck." Kim, who won Park Sang-hyun's cherished prize through the first-come, first-served "No Fun Lottery," saw the caddie bag gift delivered by Park Sang-hyun himself and said, "I didn't have this chance to win, but I'm happy to be here at the golf course." In particular, Park Sang-hyun drew attention by putting "surprise gifts" such as medicines and cosmetics in various pockets in his caddie bag. The caddie bag prize delivered by Park Sang-hyun on the day was a caddie bag that shared two wins last year. Coincidentally, Kim watched Park Sang-hyun's game in the third round of the tournament the previous day. Park Sang-hyun also delivered active fan service by signing a caddie bag and responding to photoshoots with Kim. Kim also expressed his feelings, saying, "I am grateful to receive autographs, pictures, and good gifts from players who have a deep relationship with this tournament." The gift event of professional golfers, which was first introduced last year and attracted the attention of the golf world, brought great joy to 21 winners this year as well. On the 3rd, the final day of the competition, Park Sang-hyun's caddie bag, last year's winner Moon Do-yup's driver, Lee Tae-hee's wedge, the most wins (2 wins) in the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, Ok Tae-hoon's hybrid for last year's Genesis, and Jang Yoo-bin's wedge for 2024 Genesis were delivered to the winners. Unforgettable memories for both players and fans were made at this year's GS Caltex Maekyung Open. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: Home Shopping, SSM Forge Alliance Targeting Women in 40s-60s - Seoul Economic Daily
content:
GS Shop and The Fresh Partner to Overcome Profit Slowdown
Joint Marketing Boosts Average Spending and Payment Volume
Instant Delivery and Private Brands Accelerate Business Model Shift
NS Home Shopping Eyes Synergy Through Homeplus Express Acquisition Home shopping operators and supermarket chains (SSM) are building an alliance based on their shared core customer base of women in their 40s to 60s, expanding synergies between the two channels. With both sectors facing slowing profitability, they are pursuing strategies that blur channel boundaries to lock in loyal customers. Harim Group, which recently expressed intent to acquire Homeplus Express, is also seen as targeting synergies through business ties with its subsidiary NS Home Shopping. According to the retail industry Monday, GS The Fresh, the SSM business of GS Retail, and GS Shop, its TV home shopping arm, are conducting the industry's first such collaboration. The two channels are carrying out joint customer relationship management (CRM) marketing based on shared customer data through an integrated membership program, encouraging cross-channel purchases. To this end, the two channels introduced a benefit in January that awards additional points to customers who make purchases on both platforms simultaneously. A representative joint marketing initiative is the "15Days" event, which runs from the 1st to the 15th of each month. All customers who pay via GS Pay at either GS Shop or GS The Fresh and participate in the event receive points. This month, the companies are expanding promotions, including offering GS The Fresh discount coupons to customers who participate in the GS Shop app attendance event. The effects of the joint marketing were immediate. Based on event participants, the monthly average payment amount in the first quarter of this year rose 29.0% compared with December last year. The number of event participants also increased 23.3%, while average spending per customer rose 4.8%. GS Retail is pursuing this strategy to find a new breakthrough for its home shopping and SSM businesses, both of which face slowing profitability. Despite steady revenue growth, GS The Fresh posted operating profit of just 27.1 billion won last year, an operating margin of 1.6%. GS Shop saw its revenue and operating profit continue to decline, with operating profit falling below 100 billion won last year. The similarity of the two channels' core customer bases is also cited as a reason management chose the "collaboration" card. "Both home shopping and supermarkets have a high proportion of female customers in their 40s to 60s, so we focused on the possibility of integrated marketing using common customer data," an industry official said. "The strategy aims to strengthen customer lock-in effects by removing barriers between channels, expanding cross-purchases, and encouraging repeat purchases by the same customers." The companies are also pursuing business linkage beyond joint marketing. A representative example is the introduction of an "omnichannel" service that allows customers to pick up GS Shop orders at nearby GS The Fresh stores. The cooperation model is also expected to evolve toward maximizing synergy between offline fresh food and broadcast and mobile channels by incorporating quick commerce. The industry views Harim Group's bid to acquire Homeplus Express as similarly aimed at leveraging synergies between the two channels, using Homeplus Express's 293 stores nationwide to build an instant delivery system and expand product competitiveness and customer touchpoints. "The combination of home shopping and SSM is a key means of transforming the business model, enabling expansion into the short-distance instant delivery market and securing omnichannel competitiveness based on online-offline integration," an NS Home Shopping official said. "In particular, the expansion of private brand (PB) products was limited due to broadcast time constraints, but securing offline bases will allow us to absorb volumes unsold through home shopping and fully develop related businesses." AI-translated from Korean. Quotes from foreign sources are based on Korean-language reports and may not reflect exact original wording.

title: Mars, Machavern Deliver Turner’s First GS Win in Nearly A Year - Sportscar365
content: Turner Motorsport claimed 50th GS win for BMW in Michelin Pilot Challenge at Laguna Seca… It took just three races for the new driver pairing of Dillon Machavern and Luca Mars, two past IMSA Michelin Pilot Challenge champions, to taste victory for Turner Motorsport and BMW as a collective unit. Machavern and Mars topped the GS class of Saturday’s WeatherTech Laguna Seca Raceway 120. They drove the No. 95 BMW M4 GT4 EVO to a 4.083-second triumph over the No. 2 CSM Porsche 718 GT4 RS Clubsport shared by Gordon Scully and Morgan Burkhard, with pole-sitter Nate Cicero and co-driver Robert Noaker taking third in the No. 13 McCumbee McAleer Racing Ford Mustang GT4. Machavern qualified eighth fastest but had moved up to third place behind the No. 13 Mustang and the No. 12 RAFA Racing Toyota GR Supra GT4 EVO2 when almost the entire GS field pitted under caution 56 minutes into the two-hour race. Mars exited the pits as the effective race leader, pursued by Noaker and Michael Cooper in the No. 44 Ibiza Farm Motorsport McLaren Artura GT4 that he took over from Moisey Uretsky. The three cars circulated in close proximity for most of the final hour as the race ran caution-free to the finish. In the closing stages, Mars was able to slightly progress away, and the pressure eased when Noaker eased his pace to save fuel and Cooper was forced to make a splash-and-go stop. Burkhard then passed a slowing Noaker for second place on the last lap. Mars was also conserving fuel, but it was not an issue in the end as he, Machavern, and Turner scored BMW’s 50th race win in GS class competition and 92nd overall in Pilot Challenge. The victory was the eighth in the series for Machavern and the fourth for Mars and the first for the team in series competition since Mid-Ohio last June. Mason Filippi and Bryson Morris co-drove the No. 33 Bryan Herta Autosport Hyundai Elantra N TCR to the TCR class win. It was a 1-2 finish for BHA, as Preston Brown and Denis Dupont rebounded from an early spin and a puncture to claim second place in the No. 76 Hyundai, a full lap behind their teammates. The Herta team now has five wins at three different tracks, the first team in history to do so. At Laguna Seca, the win joins its four previous triumphs in 2020, 2021, 2023 and 2025. The victory – which was the ten in Pilot Challenge competition for 2025 TCR champion Filippi and the second for Morris – was nowhere as easy as the result would indicate despite the fact that the No. 33 car basically led from the start to the checkered flag. Filippi took the pole and held the top spot through his first stint before handing off to Morris just prior to the halfway point of the 120-minute race. The biggest challenge to Morris came from the No. 99 Victor Gonzalez Racing CUPRA Leon VZ TCR, but Tyler Gonzalez, who shared the car with Franco Girolami, sustained a punctured left rear tire with 37 minutes remaining while running within a second of the lead and fell to seventh. Celso Neto and Rafael Reis then looked set to take second place in the No. 77 Stallion Motorsports w/GOU CUPRA, only for the car to stop on course with just two minutes to go. That left Dupont and Brown to collect the runner-up laurels, followed in third place by Karl Wittmer and LP Montour in the No. 93 MMG Honda Civic FL5 TCR. title: "Mulligan at a Pro Tour?": KGA Ruling Sparks Controversy at GS Caltex Maekyung Open - Seoul Economic Daily
content:
GS Caltex Maekyung Open, Round 3, Hole 7
Heo In-hoe Plays Provisional Ball After Tee Shot
On-Site Check: Fore-Caddie Moved the Ball
Referee: "Tee Shot Canceled, Play the Provisional"
Players: "Effectively a Mulligan—At a Pro Event?"
Committee: "Wrong Ruling, But Too Late to Correct" A "mulligan" — a second-chance shot typically seen only in friendly rounds among amateur golfers — made an appearance at a professional golf tournament. The incident occurred at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open (total purse of 1.3 billion won) held at Namseoul Country Club in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd. The tournament is co-sanctioned by the Korea Golf Association (KGA) and the Asian Tour. The incident took place on the 7th hole (par 4). Heo In-hoe's tee shot headed toward the woods on the right side of the fairway. Believing his tee shot might have gone into the out-of-bounds (OB) area, Heo played a provisional ball. The problem arose next. When he walked up to where the ball had landed, a fore-caddie standing ahead had picked up Heo's ball and placed it inside the course. Heo argued, "Since the fore-caddie moved my ball, there's no way to know whether it went out of bounds or not." After deliberation, the on-site rules officials canceled Heo's original tee shot and instructed him to play the provisional ball that was on the fairway. Heo's score on the hole was a par, reaching the green in two and two-putting. Once word of the incident spread, controversy erupted among the players. The gist of their complaints: "Canceling the original tee shot is essentially a 'mulligan.' How can this happen at a professional tournament?" A mulligan refers to an opportunity given to a playing partner, without penalty, to hit another shot when their shot is poor. It is not in the Rules of Golf and is used only in friendly rounds among amateur golfers. In fact, this was not a particularly complicated rules situation. Since Heo's ball was moved by an outside influence (the fore-caddie), the ball should have been replaced at its original spot. If that spot is unknown, it must be estimated. The rules official should have confirmed the original location of the ball with the fore-caddie and then determined whether Heo's ball was OB. If it was OB, Heo should have played his fourth stroke with the provisional ball. If it was not OB, he should have continued play with his second stroke from the original spot. The on-site rules officials instructed Heo to play the provisional ball on the fairway. This means they had ruled that Heo's ball was OB. In that case, Heo's score should have been a double bogey — four strokes to reach the green and two putts. In the end, the rules official made a "wrong ruling." The tournament committee reportedly explained, "There was a 'wrong ruling' by the on-site referee. However, by the time we became aware of it, three holes had already passed. The situation could not be reversed, so play continued as it was." But questions remain as to whether three holes later is truly too late to correct the error. Hole-by-hole scores can be revised even when the final scorecard is submitted after the round. Moreover, neither a rules official nor the committee has any authority to cancel a player's stroke. Since Heo followed the referee's instruction, he does not receive the general penalty (two strokes) for playing from a wrong place when he played the ball on the fairway. However, this does not mean the penalty for his ball going into the OB area is waived, or that the preceding stroke itself is canceled. When asked for an official position on the incident, the Korea Golf Association said, "We are currently listening to the views of all parties involved." AI-translated from Korean. title: There are 18 holes left before the winner of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open is born. With Song Min.. - 매일경제
content:
There are 18 holes left before the winner of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open is born. With Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu, who recorded 10-under 203, tied for the lead, participants are raising their desire to win more this year. The new preparation for the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, which is famous for its extraordinary respect for past champions, is the champion Caddy Bibb (a vest worn by Caddy). The caddy bibs of Moon Do-yeop, Lee Tae-hee, Park Sang-hyun, Jung Chan-min, and Kim Hong-taek are engraved on the star symbolizing the winner. The all-time winners have expressed a desire to add a star to their caddy bib. Moon Do-yeop, Lee Tae-hee, and Park Sang-hyun said, "I was surprised to see a caddy bib different from last year. I'm really grateful to the organizing committee of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open for caring about the winners." Players who have not won yet expressed their desire to have a champion-only caddy bib someday. Song Min-hyuk, Cho Min-gyu, and Kim Min-gyu emphasized, "The GS Caltex Maekyung Open is a competition that everyone wants to win," adding, "We will do our best to engrave a star on Cadib." The respect for the champions does not stop there. The organizing committee of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open provided protocol services along with exclusive vehicles to past winners. The champions' satisfaction with BMW's official dealer Handok Motors' shopper service was high. Defending champion Moon Do-yeop, 2024 winner Kim Hong-taek, and 2023 winner Jung Chan-min used the shopper service. They said, "We are playing this tournament really comfortably with the consideration of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open Organizing Committee. The players are really envious as they wear championship-only vehicles. I'm really grateful for caring about the winners," he explained. Song Min-hyuk, Cho Min-gyu and Lee Tae-hee, who were in the championship group on the final day of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open, hit their first tee shot at 10:21 a.m. on the first hole. Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu are trying to win the tournament for the first time. Lee Tae-hee, who won the previous two titles, is aiming for her first three wins in the history of the tournament. Moon Do-yeop, who was tied for fourth at 6-under 207 until the third day, is aiming for his second consecutive victory at the South Seoul CC. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: Retaking the hegemony of overseas national athletes has faced another crisis. Amid the strong perfor.. - 매일경제
content:
Retaking the hegemony of overseas national athletes has faced another crisis. Amid the strong performance of Korean players at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open, Ian Snyman (South Africa), the only player from overseas to rank in the top 10, has built up his pride. Snyman cut four shots in the third round of the tournament held at Nam Seoul CC (par 71) in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd, tied for seventh with a 5-under 208 overall. He is five strokes behind co-leaders Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu (hereinafter, 10-under 203). Of the 52 foreign nationalities from 18 countries who participated in the tournament, Snyman was the only one in the top 10 and created an opportunity to seek a come-from-behind victory in the final four rounds. Snyman, who started at the 10th hole on the day, made an eagle putt about 12 meters away from the 14th hole (par 5), and was able to reach the top 10 with a birdie putt that was difficult to climb down on the 9th hole (par 5). After the game, Snyman said, "I tried to play more conservatively than I did the day before. The strategy rather led to a better score. The biggest goal was to keep the ball under the hole all the time, most of which did that well. He said that there were about two mistakes, which led to a view of all of them. Snyman, who turned professional in 2018 and has been consistently active in the Asian Tour since 2020, has been an emerging dark horse, finishing second in the New Zealand Open last year. But he has never won a championship yet. The GS Caltex Maekyung Open also failed to make the cut in both 2024 and last year. Still, the GS Caltex Maekyung Open is meaningful to Snyman. Snyman's husband is Korean. "My sister came to Korea 15 years ago to teach English, and she met her husband here, got married, and now has two children," Snyman said. Snyman, who met her sister every time she participated in a competition in Korea, held a "surprise event" for her this year. Snyman said, "I brought my brother to caddie. As we don't see each other often in a year, I suggested to my brother, "Do you want to go to Korea together while caddying me?" and gladly accepted. The idea was to surprise my family so much that my parents in South Africa didn't even know it, but as soon as my sister saw my brother, I cried. I almost cried too," he said, revealing the behind-the-scenes story. This year's GS Caltex Maekyung Open, where three siblings are together in Korea, is a great help to Snyman. He said, "My sister has a good family here, but she misses her hometown. I was glad that my family could get together like this. I think it will be memorable for a long time to see my brother happy with each putt, he said. "That's how special this tournament is to me. Golf plays a role in connecting the family to the whole world," he said with a smile. Snyman, who is doing well in this competition with his family's support on his back, said, "I have no intention of changing the strategy I have used so far. The putting is really good. "I've been good throughout this tournament, so I hope you continue to make good putts on the last day," he said. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: This Spider Launches at 130 G’s (Humans Black Out at 9) - AOL.com
content: Some predators chase. Others ambush. And then there are the ones that shoot. Across the animal kingdom, a surprising number of species have evolved ways to hit prey from a distance — firing water jets, venom sprays, sticky silk, or even microscopic harpoons. These aren’t gimmicks. They’re highly specialized hunting systems designed to solve one core problem: how to catch prey that is faster than the predator. Many rely on what scientists call latch-mediated spring actuation (LaMSA) — a way to store energy and release it instantly, like a biological trigger. The result is explosive speed, extreme precision, and in some cases, forces that rival human-made machines. From fish that snipe insects out of the air to snails that fire venom-loaded harpoons, these predators have weaponized the laws of physics. While many creatures, such as bombardier beetles and spitting cobras, “shoot” to defend themselves, these elite marksmen use their abilities offensively, actively hunting and capturing their next meal. If you thought fish only hunted underwater, the archerfish will quickly prove you wrong. This aquatic outlaw can knock insects off branches up to 5 feet above the water by firing a precise jet of water from its mouth. But it’s not just about force or power; it’s about physics and precision. Because light bends when it passes from air to water, the fish’s view of the insect is distorted. To hit its mark, the archerfish must account for this “refraction” before firing. This skill isn’t purely instinctual, either. Young archerfish practice to refine their aim, eventually achieving near-perfect accuracy, especially when they position themselves directly beneath their target. While many spiders wait for prey to get stuck in their webs, the spitting spider actively immobilizes its prey. In a fraction of a second, the spider sprays a specialized mixture of liquid silk and venom from its fangs while it rapidly vibrates its head, causing the silky streams to form a zigzagging net in the air. This sticky, toxic mesh pins insects to whatever surface they’re on. It’s fast, precise, and extremely effective. Unlike other eight-eyed spider species, spitting spiders only have six eyes. While most shrimp are scavengers, the pistol shrimp is a gunslinger of the sea. Unlike its relatives, this impressive hunter doesn’t kill by pinching; it uses physics to hunt. When the shrimp slams its oversized claw shut, it ejects a high-speed jet of water that creates a cavitation bubble. This bubble is a tiny pocket of low pressure that collapses almost instantly, triggering a massive release of energy. The pistol shrimp may only be 1.5 inches long, but it can move its claws at 60 miles per hour. The collapse of this bubble is so violent that it produces a shockwave louder than a gunshot, stunning or killing small prey from a distance. For a tiny fraction of a second, the temperature inside the collapsing bubble can reach nearly 8,700 degrees Fahrenheit (4,800 degrees Celsius). The energy release is so intense it actually produces a brief flash of light. The shrimp’s claw features a unique spiral, crack-resistant structure designed to withstand the repeated stress of these underwater “explosions.” Materials scientists are actively studying the pistol shrimp’s claw to develop stronger, more impact-resistant armor and helmets for humans. The velvet worm may appear soft, slow, and relatively harmless, but it is a surprisingly effective predator. Using specialized nozzles near its mouth, it sprays twin jets of slime at a speed reaching nearly 5 feet per second. The slime hardens in the air as the jets oscillate rapidly, and the movement ensures that the slimy webbing covers a large area to make it nearly impossible for an insect to escape. Once its prey is immobilized, the worm dissolves the victim’s insides and consumes the liquefied remains. Velvet worms typically hunt woodlice, crickets, and spiders. The worm also eats the dried strands of slime afterward, ensuring that no resources are wasted after the energy-intensive hunt. Unlike other spiders that build stationary webs and wait for prey to get caught, the ogre-faced spider actively casts its net like a tiny eight-legged fisherman. Hanging upside down from a single silk line, the spider holds a small, rectangular frame of highly elastic silk between its front legs. It uses its specialized slit sensilla or vibration sensors to detect the movements of insects walking or flying nearby. When an insect comes within range, the spider lunges, stretching the net to several times its original size and snapping it over the prey. Two of the ogre-faced spider’s eyes face forward and are much bigger than the rest, giving it excellent low-light night vision. The cone snail moves at a literal snail’s pace, yet its strike is one of the fastest and most lethal in nature. Instead of chasing its prey, this snail uses its hollow, venom-tipped tooth—essentially a built-in biological harpoon—to strike with incredible speed. It happens in less than one millisecond, even faster than the blink of a human eye. Once the harpoon hits, the snail injects a complex cocktail of neurotoxins that shuts down the prey’s nervous system instantly. Some cone snail species can launch their harpoon in less than 100 microseconds. Some species, like the geography cone (Conus geographus), are so toxic that a single sting can be fatal to humans. This species is sometimes called the “cigarette snail” due to a grim myth: if you are stung, you’ll only have enough time to smoke one last cigarette before you die. While this may be an exaggeration, the venom does act extremely quickly. Instead of spinning traditional webs, the bolas spider uses a specialized “fishing” technique to catch its prey. Instead of a net, it creates a single silk line tipped with a heavy and sticky glob called a bolas. The bolas’ incredibly sticky texture allows it to bypass a moth’s slippery scales and adhere directly to its body. The bolas spider creates silk with a sticky texture. ©Judy Gallagher / CC BY 2.0 / Wikimedia Commons – Original / License The spider releases a scent that mimics the pheromones of a female moth. When a male moth approaches in search of a mate, the spider swings the bolas in a circular motion to snag the moth out of the air. The chameleon is a long-range predator that relies on mechanical advantage rather than raw muscle power to catch its prey. Its long tongue doesn’t simply reach out to snatch up an insect; instead, the chameleon launches it from its mouth like a high-speed projectile. This mechanism relies on stored energy rather than rapid muscle movement, allowing it to function even in cooler temperatures. Chameleons have a unique physiology that allows them to “wind up” energy in their tongue’s tissue so they can snap it out at speeds of 60 miles per hour in a fraction of a second. The tip of the chameleon’s tongue has a suction cup-like pouch coated with saliva that is about 400 times thicker than human saliva. This special coating ensures that the bond is strong enough to pull the prey back, even if it is heavy or struggling. Rather than waiting for an insect to fly into its web, the slingshot spider transforms its entire web into a loaded spring. It constructs a conical web and pulls the center tight using a tension line attached to a nearby surface. By pulling back and holding the web under extreme tension, the spider stores potential energy, essentially turning itself into a living bow and arrow. When the spider senses prey, it releases the tension line, sending itself and its web catapulting forward at over 130 Gs (for perspective, humans typically black out at around 9 Gs). The sudden launch collapses the web over the prey, entangling it before it can react. Slingshot spiders shoot their own bodies at their prey. ©LOPEZ André / CC BY-SA 4.0 / Wikimedia Commons – Original / License The same silk structures that provide the tension also act as shock absorbers, bringing the spider to a safe stop at the end of its flight. The post This Spider Launches at 130 G’s (Humans Black Out at 9) appeared first on A-Z Animals.

title: Dushanbe Grand Slam, Day 2 - Preliminaries - IJF
content: The top seed being a member of the host nation’s team created quite a fervour, with packed stands from hours before the first contests began. Muhiddin Asadulloev (TJK) could have buckled under the pressure but his home crowd gave him energy and support and he played to it excellently. Despite almost conceding a yuko in his first contest, overturned on review, he powered through the rounds and into the final, Azerbaijan’s Rashid Mammadaliyev unable to disturb him too much in their semi-final. Karen Galstian (RUS) will meet Asadulloev in their shared last contest of the day, the whole arena backing their man. Ankhzaya Lavjargal began the day as the second seed but it was Galstian who toppled him ahead of the final block. The top two athletes in the category, according to the world ranking, were both from Mongolia, Enkhriilen Lkhagvatogoo and Gankhaich Bold. The first has two word medals, the second has one; the race to gold could be tight. Third seed Lubjana Piovesana was the athlete most likely to upset the natural order. She knows what it means to compete at the highest level and is working towards her second Olympic qualification having placed 5th in Paris in 2024. In fact Piovesana pushed Bold into golden score in their semi-final, looking strong until Bold made space and secured the ippon win via a clean shime-waza. The Mongolian derby was guaranteed once Lkhagvatogoo dominated in her own semi-final, sending Kim (KOR) away with three penalties in under 3 minutes. Just as Tajikistan had their athlete atop the -73 kg draw, Somon Makhmadbekov (TJK) made it a clean sweep in the men’s categories for the hosts as he too arrived as a top seed on day 2. With Olympic and world bronze medals in his trophy cabinet, his ascent looked fairly safe, especially on home soil, but little can be guaranteed in judo; his route was not easy. Makhmadbekov passed Baisiev (RUS) with two small scores, Askar Narkulov (KAZ) on penalties and then Alpha Djalo by a single yuko. He made it to the final but it had been precarious. Could Bernd Fasching (AUT) upset the crowd in the final block and capitalise on the Tajik athlete’s ‘need’ to win at home? Fasching, the number two seed, had a smoother day than Makhmadbekov, winning his first contest by a yuko but following up with two ippon wins to reach the final. Top seed Ida Eriksson (SWE) had to withdraw due to illness and this left a gaping hole in the draw, one that had to be filled by one judoka or another. It could have been Jana Cvjetko (CRO) who stepped forward as she had been placed in Eriksson’s pool. She did win the quarter-final but could not overcome Poland’s Aleksandra Kowalewska who won their semi-final with an inescapable shime-waza. On the other half of the draw, there was clear dominance from Austria’s double Olympic and double world medallist Michaela Polleres. She won every contest by ippon on the way to the final. Kowalewska would have to be on top form to match the power of Polleres; this final looked set to go only one way. It has been a great preliminary session for the hosts, two men’s categories and two men’s finals. The women’s team is still battling for their first medal but in Tajikistan, all medals won belong to the athlete, the team and the country. 03. title: Giuffrida: “The Atmosphere in Dushanbe Gave Me Power” - IJF
content: So, at 31 years old and with limited time left on the competition circuit, why did Giuffrida choose the 2026 Dushanbe Grand Slam? She has almost every medal an athlete could ever want. The Olympic qualification period is about to begin and one could be forgiven for thinking that maybe that would be the focus. “I wanted to compete in Dushanbe before I retire because many people told me that there is nowhere like Tajikistan, and they were right! I wish that every judo enthusiast, once in their life, will experience this. I am glad that Dushanbe will host the World Judo Masters in 2026, so I can return and experience how nice the local people are once again. After the Europeans, I felt I had nothing left, but the atmosphere here gave me power. The Europeans were exhausting because there are plenty of great athletes and every opponent is strong. However, emotionally I was one hundred percent well and after a short rest I wanted to judo again and again.” Odette Giuffrida won gold in Dushanbe, her 35th World Judo Tour medal (including the Olympic Games) and continues to shine. Her response though is simple, “Nobody knows what will happen after LA28, as I am not getting any younger, but I do know one thing: after I land in Italy, I will go out for dinner with my family and celebrate with some pizza!” 03. title: Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu occupied the top of the leader board of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Op.. - 매일경제
content:
Song Min-hyuk and Cho Min-gyu occupied the top of the leader board of the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. Song Min-hyuk shot a 5-under 66 in the third round of the tournament held at Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam, Gyeonggi Province on the 2nd. Song Min-hyuk, who had an interim total of 10-under 203, tied for the lead with Cho Min-gyu. Lee Tae-hee, the only third-place player in the team, beat Lee by three shots. Song Min-hyuk, who started the game tied for fifth place, reduced five strokes by combining six birdies and one bogey. Song Min-hyuk, who tied for the lead, had a golden opportunity to win his first professional title at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. "Overall, it was a good day," Song Min-hyuk said. "I'm happy to start the final day game tied for the lead." Song Min-hyuk, who won the tournament's best amateur in 2022 and 2023, when he played for the national team, is showing a strong performance at the South Seoul CC, where the GS Caltex Maekyung Open is held. The best record ever was the runner-up, and this year he is aiming for the championship. Song Min-hyuk said, "The competition I wanted to win the most since I was young is the GS Caltex Maekyung Open," adding, "I don't want to miss it as it's a difficult opportunity to catch." I will focus on it until the end so that I can continue the good atmosphere," he stressed. Cho Min-gyu, the runner-up of the tournament in 2011, 2020, and 2022, also did not hide his desire to win. Cho Min-gyu said, "It's not a victory that I can say I want to do, but I want to finish well," adding, "You shouldn't be in a hurry in South Seoul CC. I will calm down and do a good job on the final day," he said. Moon Do-yeop, the winner of last year's event, also inflated his hopes of winning for the second consecutive year. Moon Do-yeop, who posted a 6-under 207, tied for fourth. Kim Min-kyu, who plays at the LIV Golf Korean Golf Club, also did well. Kim Min-kyu, who is tied for fourth place with Moon Do-yeop, will try to win the come-from-behind victory on the final day. Kim Sung-hyun and Choi Jin-ho wrote a 5-under 208 to finish the third round tied for seventh. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: All-time championship competition is taking place at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. On the second.. - 매일경제
content:
All-time championship competition is taking place at the 45th GS Caltex Maekyung Open. On the second day, Lee Tae-hee, Jang Yoo-bin, Shin Sang-hoon, and Cho Min-gyu were tied for the lead, and 47 players were within a five-shot gap. The third round, in which the main character who will lift the trophy, will be held on the 2nd at Nam Seoul CC in Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do. Lee Tae-hee, Jang Yoo-bin, Shin Sang-hoon, and Cho Min-gyu showed the best performance on the first and second days. The four players, who recorded 6-under 136 for two days, dominated the top of the leaderboard. The most notable name is Lee Tae-hee, the two-time winner of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. Lee Tae-hee, who is also the main character of the tournament's second consecutive victory for the first time in history, is trying to win three times. Lee Tae-hee, who hit 6 under par on the first day, wrote even par on the second day. Lee Tae-hee was satisfied even though she failed to reduce the number of pars. This is because he remained in the lead without shaking even in the face of strong winds. Lee Tae-hee said, "I had five bogeys, but I recorded even par with one eagle and three birdies," adding, "As the shot and putt are not bad, I am looking forward to the rest of the round. I will prepare well and finish the tournament well," he said. Jang Yoo-bin, who returned to Korea after playing in LIV Golf last year, showed his performance in 2024, when he won the Korea Professional Golf Association (KPGA) Tour Genesis. "The current shot is similar to 2024, when golf is the best," Jang Yoo-bin said. "If the putt goes well, I think we can perform better than tied for fourth in 2024 and tied for eighth in 2023." Shin Sang-hoon and Cho Min-gyu also did not hide their desire to become the champion of the GS Caltex Maekyung Open. The two players expressed their determination to engrave their names on the list of winners of this tournament. The two players vowed to "do their best to compete for the championship in front of the cloud crowd." Kim Min-kyu and Kim Sung-hyun, who are active in LIV Golf and the PGA Tour, also did well. The two players tied for fifth place with 5-under 137. Moon Do-yeop, the winner of last year's event, tied for 13th at 4-under 138. Moon Do-yeop, who lost one shot on the first day and was on the verge of missing the cut, hit 5 under par on the second day, setting the stage for his second consecutive victory. The result of deciding to pass the cut was decided to be one over 143. The gap with the co-leader group is seven shots. As we don't know what will happen in the difficult South Seoul CC, all the players who made the cut will try to raise the ranking on the third day. ※ This article was translated using AI technology for reader convenience. title: -52 kg: The Unshakable Odette Giuffrida - IJF
content: Aleksandra Kaleta was the judoka tasked with challenging Italy’s champion and it would be a steep hill to climb. However, with a medal and valuable world ranking points already guaranteed, an open judo ‘discussion’ could yield an unexpected opportunity for her. The contest was closer than Giuffrida might have liked. It was clear that the Polish judoka had done her homework, arriving prepared to oppose the Italian champion’s preferred grips and attacks. However, Giuffrida is not new to well-drilled adversaries and she calmly dominated, step by step; an understated confidence that left Kaleta collecting unhelpful penalties. Odette Giuffrida adds a sixth grand slam gold to her impressive resumé. Odette Giufrrida was engaging and bubbly after the final, "I wanted to compete in Dushanbe because many people told me that there is nothing like Tajikistan, and they were right! The atmosphere here gave me power. I am glad that Dushanbe will host the Masters in 2026 so I can return and experience how nice the local people are once again." In the first contest for a bronze medal Gal-od Tserentogtokh (MGL) was up against Italy’s Kenya Perna, both hunting a first World Judo Tour medal. It was a close contest but rule infringements from the Mongolian judoka gave Perna the edge and she took the medal on penalties. She ran off the mat into her coach’s arms, emotional and energised by winning her first medal at this level. In the second contest for a bronze medal Nandin-Erdene Myagmarsuren (MGL) met Mukhayyo Akhmatova (UZB in the middle of the tatami. As the first minute disappeared Myagmarsuren hooked in with a ko-uchi-gake and scored an important yuko. Akhmatova got straight down to work in an effort to equalise but Myagmarsuren was ready to defend her bounty. Nothing changed in the scoreline and as the gong sounded, the Mongolian delegation could celebrate another medal on their growing tally. 03. title: -66 kg: Emomali Excels at Home - IJF
content: In the final he faced Azizbek Ortikov (UZB) who had delivered an excellent run of wins in the preliminary session. Ortikov wasn’t ready for Emomali and the energy of the crowd though and Tajikistan’s hero of the day countered for what was very nearly ippon in the first exchange. Ortikov had a second chance, just, but Emomali was not about to lose at home, not today. Although each judoka racked up two penalties, making defence more complicated, Emomali managed to hold is ground, conceding no scores and protecting his own. The Tajik national anthem would ring out in the  arena on day one in Dushanbe. Nurali Emomali, happy and relieved to have won the medal, said, “I cannot be thankful enough to the Tajikistan Judo Federation, to the Tajikistan Olympic Committee and to the people of Tajikistan who always support me. During the final, I believed in my judo continuously even after the first scored was changed and with the support of the fans, I knew I could not lose today. This glory tonight is for them.” In the first bronze medal contest Vadym Chernov (UKR) faced Iago Abuladze (RUS) and it was a tense from the outset. Abuladze took control early, scoring a yuko and then later using a soto-makikomi to add a waza-ari to the scoreboard. Chernov was able to recoup a yuko of his own but with seconds left on the clock, that was all he could do. Charging at Abuladze for one last attempt, he was countered and thrown fore a second waza-ari, sealing his own fate. The second bronze medal could join Abuladze’s in Russia, at the hands of German Kobets, or it could be won by Nizami Imranov, competing for Azerbaijan’s first medal of the Dushanbe Grand Slam 2026. The answer took time to develop, a close contest ensued with both fighters bringing attacking judo to the tatami. In the end though, it was Kobets who scored with an uchi-mata well inside the lats minute of normal time and converted it into ippon on the ground. The Russian Federation had secured both bronze medals in this category. 03. May. 2026 / The Dushanbe Grand Slam 2026 is already coming to a ... 03. title: Dushanbe Delivers Again and Again - IJF
content: The local organisers have shown, year after year, that they have a federation workforce that loves judo, supported by volunteers who are totally in tune with the rhythm of judo too. This is the foundation of the Dushanbe Grand Slam, a unique event on the IJF calendar and a reminder of the power of shared passion. The national anthem of the republic of Tajikistan was heard, thanks to the effort and skill of Nurali Emomali. He is already number two in the world ranking and in Dushanbe he consolidated this, showing that he is willing to give every ounce of energy for the result, for his federation and for the judo fans that filled the arena. Emomali was not the only Tajik medallist on day one; Loiq Kudbudinov gave the performance of his life to win his first WJT medal, a bronze fuelled by the encouraging noise from the stands. To feel the power of a home-grown medal in Dushanbe is to feel something special and Kudbudinov felt every beat of it. The Russian Federation took pole position on the medal table at the end of the day, thanks to Ayub Bliev (-60 kg) and Olga Mukhina’s (-57 kg) golds and the bronze medals of Abuladze, Elkina and newcomer German Kobets. Sharp judo defined the champions and there is no doubt that their delegation will expect more of this in the two remaining days. Mongolia and Italy took the last two titles, Anudari Jamsran (-48 kg) and Odette Giuffrida (-52 kg) delivering dominant strategies, despite being at opposite ends of their careers. This is an interesting dynamic in elite judo that points to a potential longevity that must be admired. Judo is hard and it is complicated to reach the top level but teenagers an veterans alike have the possibility to be at the top of their game, with hard work and a great support team. Jamsran and Giuffrida proved this today. Day two brings the middleweights to the Qasri Tennis arena and the action can be found live on JudoTV from midday local time. Tune in to enjoy the uniqueness of the Dushanbe Grand Slam. 03. May. 2026 / The Dushanbe Grand Slam 2026 is already coming to a ... 03. title: BMW F 450 GS review: GS WHAT! - BusinessLine
content:
BMW F 450 GS
																															| Photo Credit:
											Kaizad Adil Darukhanawala BMW Motorrad’s GS lineup is easily its most prolific one, popular in India as well as the rest of the world; the G series had a single-cylinder engine, the F series carries parallel-twins, while the R series features BMW’s famous boxer-twins. It’s a combination of history, versatility and desirability that enables BMW to sell this family of do-it-all motorcycles by and to the droves. And now, we have the new F 450 GS, a motorcycle that replaces the G 310 GS while also superseding it in almost every aspect. Designed by BMW but made by TVS Motor in India, the F 450 GS is a premium Motorrad machine in terms of build quality and equipment. It gets a 6.5-inch TFT screen connected to a rotary-scroll dial on the left side of the handlebar, a typical BMW touch, and the terminologies used in it are the same as the bigger GSes, too. There’s an X-shaped headlight with all-LED elements, a detail that again connects it directly to its bigger siblings. The clutch and alternator covers are made of magnesium alloy which helps keep the motor’s and overall weight down. And there are three variants: Base ( ₹4.7 lakh), Exclusive ( ₹4.9 lakh) and GS Trophy ( ₹5.3 lakh). I rode the last one, which comes with all the bells and whistles, including an easy-ride clutch (ERC). A lot more on that later. Headline numbers are as follows: a brand-new 420cc parallel-twin with a 135-degree crankpin offset makes 48 bhp at 8750 rpm and 4.42 kg-m at 6750 rpm, both sent to the road via a 6-speed gearbox. The motorcycle weighs 178 kg with a 14-litre fuel tank and a seat height of 845 mm. It comes with 19-inch front and 17-inch rear wheels with 100/90- and 130/80-section tyres, standard sizes for all-purpose motorcycles. Suspension travel stands at 180 mm at both ends, with a ground clearance of 220 mm. And BMW claims a top speed of 165 kph for it, which is more than is necessary in the real world. Among electronic rider assists are a ride-by-wire throttle, a quickshifter, four ride modes (Rain, Road, Enduro and Enduro Pro), traction control, dynamic-brake control, engine drag-torque control and lean-sensitive ABS. Most of these features are standard across the variants, though some additions are available only on the Exclusive, while the GS Trophy gets all of them and then some. The F 450 GS also comes with heated grips as standard across all variants which, I think, is a bit too much in a mostly superheated country like ours. And now for the most fun part. Out on the road, far away from glorious spec-sheet claims and sky-high expectations, the F 450 GS left me nonplussed. Yes, it felt like a full-size motorcycle, roomy and comfortable yet manageable. But, for me, the ERC took away a significant part of the involvement of riding a motorcycle. Was it for a beginner, an expert or someone in between? It’s a centrifugal clutch that engages at 2700 rpm; until then, at a standstill, you can row up and down the gearbox without touching the clutch lever, and the motorcycle won’t budge. Indeed, in the spirit of journalistic inquiry, I even made a standing start in sixth gear and the F 450 GS simply rolled away, whining like a big scooter. This is probably why the clutch-lever action is on the harder side; the internals necessarily have to be stouter to deal with less-than-considerate riding. And after the screen is done flashing a message to shift to a lower gear, it’ll tell you that the clutch is overheating, too. I didn’t see the latter, though. After putting the ERC at the back of my mind, the F 450 GS seemed more like a normal motorcycle. Most apparent was how it felt so light and nimble, turning and tightening its lines at will. The brakes were strong and inspired a lot of confidence; the front brake had a very sporty and powerful feel to it, not something usually seen on a dual-purpose machine. However, what came as a surprise was that the F 450 was not exactly GS-plush; even the previous G 310 GS was cosseting in its ride quality. Sure, the F 450 GS’s suspension was strong enough to bash through everything in its path, but it transferred quite a lot of residual impacts to my body. That’s not very GS-like, I must repeat, though it was always super-stable at all speeds. Even with the GS Trophy’s adjustable suspension, which I didn’t fiddle with, I don’t see it becoming a two-wheeled cross-country sofa. I suppose it’s just a matter of getting used to it if you buy it. As for the engine, I was again left with mixed feelings. I left it in Enduro mode for the duration of my ride, with the second-last level of safety assists, and it came across as quite a lively motor. It had a bit of a high-rpm-screamer quality when I really twisted the throttle, though the overall sound was nothing exceptional, even if it was a properly quick and fast motorcycle. Nonetheless, it was fairly smooth for the most part, though vibrations did surface at various points, especially after 6500-7000 rpm. At a steady 100 kph, though, it sat at a fuss-free 5500 rpm which bodes well for long-distance riding. And it remained smooth and manageable at low speeds in traffic as well, the only niggle continuing to be the CVT-like rubber-band effect of the ERC. Overall, the F 450 GS proved to be a motorcycle that’s extremely easy to ride across a range of conditions. I only rode it off-road briefly, but I gathered that it has the hardware and electronics to acquit itself respectably in moderate off-road conditions, too. The gearshift quality, even with the quickshifter, was on the notchier side; I expect it to improve as it gets bedded in. Better tyres would be at the top of my to-do list if I ever bought the bike. Also, the quality of stickers on the bike wasn’t what I was expecting on a bike in this segment, let alone a BMW. And the lack of colour options is a letdown, too; the Base and Exclusive only come in black, while the GS Trophy is the brighter one you see here. However, with that 14-litre tank, range should easily be upwards of 300 km, which is decent enough for long hauls. But basic competence is not the point of motorcycles like these in 2026, is it? In the end, despite possessing all the right details, the F 450 GS lacked the sense of occasion that should come as standard equipment on such a motorcycle. I never once felt that I wanted this motorcycle right away. Its demeanour felt a bit out of character for a GS, honestly, and I expected it to be more than it is right now, especially given what it asks for. Does it change the mid-size game for BMW? Oh, yes. But for the segment? That’s a bit more complicated, since it doesn’t set any outright standards on its own. Also, the prices I mentioned earlier are introductory, so they will be revised upwards at some point. And BMW service costs do have a reputation of leaving wallets so empty, they echo. I’m not so sure a motorcycle made in India should be replicating what imported machines find unavoidable. But of course, there’s no denying that the F 450 is a GS — after all, it comes with those BMW roundels that are impossible to brag or argue against. If you’ve been waiting for a relatively affordable Motorrad, this is the only option you have. Not much of a choice, if you ask me. (221220-K) SEOUL: South Korean rising star Yubin Jang moved a step closer to a breakthrough victory at the GS Caltex Maekyung Open after carding a two-under-par 69 on Friday to claim a share of the halfway lead at Namseoul Country Club. The 23-year-old, regarded as one of Korean golf’s brightest talents, sits at six-under-par alongside compatriots Mingyu Cho, Sanghun Shin and two-time champion Taehee Lee in a tightly packed leaderboard, with only eight shots separating the leaders from the cut line. Cho and Shin both returned rounds of 70, while Lee signed for a 71 as the quartet headed into the weekend tied at the top. A group of eight players are just one shot behind on five-under, including New Zealand’s Nick Voke, who fired a superb 66, as well as Korean duo Minkyu Kim, a two-time Korea Open champion, and PGA Tour player Seonghyeon Kim. Jang, who competed for Iron Heads GC in the LIV Golf League last season, said the experience gained on the global circuit has helped elevate his game. “Having played on LIV Golf across a variety of courses and conditions, I found it relatively easy to adapt to the windy conditions today,” said Jang. “That experience has been a great help. Even in situations where I might normally take a more aggressive club, I’m now trying to play more steadily. I do feel that I’ve grown as a player.” Jang enjoyed a stellar 2024 season, topping the Korean Tour Order of Merit and earning Player of the Year honours, achievements that helped secure his LIV Golf opportunity. He was also part of South Korea’s gold medal-winning team at the Asian Games in 2023. “Since my days on the national team, the tournaments I’ve most wanted to win are the GS Caltex Maekyung Open and the Kolon Korea Open,” he said. “I was in contention in both 2023 and 2024, but it was disappointing not to win. Those missed opportunities have stayed with me, so I’ll do my best to play without any regrets.” Meanwhile, Voke surged into contention after making six birdies in a bogey-light round of 66. “We were feeding off each other,” said Voke after playing alongside Cho and Frenchman Julien Sale, who also carded a 66. “Very pleased with the round. Challenging golf course, but challenging conditions as well. This wind is tricky, it’s strong, it’s coming from all sorts of directions.” Defending champion Doyeob Mun also made a strong move with a 66, bouncing back from an opening 72 to sit at four-under-par heading into the weekend.
